title: Viewing History
subtitle: Your project's timeline

sections.content: Every commit you make is recorded in git's **log** — a timeline of your project's history. You can view this history anytime to see what changed, when, and who made each change.
sections.analogy: Git log is like flipping through a photo album of your project. Each page shows a snapshot (commit) with a date stamp, your name, and the note you wrote on the back. You can flip through quickly with `--oneline` or study each page in detail.
keyPoints: `git log` shows the full commit history with hashes, authors, dates, and messages
`git log --oneline` shows a compact one-line-per-commit summary
The most recent commits appear at the top of the log

sections.instruction: Let's set up a project with a commit so we can view its history.
sections.prompt: Type `git init` to initialize the repository.

sections.question: What does `git log --oneline` show?
options: A compact list: one line per commit with hash and message
sections.explanation: `git log --oneline` shows a compact summary with each commit's short hash and message on a single line. It's much easier to scan than the full `git log` output.

sections.instruction: Create a commit to save this snapshot.

onSuccess.message: Commit saved. Now let's look at your project's history.

sections.instruction: The `git log` command shows your project's timeline — every commit you've ever made, newest first.
sections.prompt: Type `git log` to view your commit history.

onSuccess.message: Every commit shows: a unique hash (that long string of letters and numbers), the author, the date, and your message. The hash is like a fingerprint — no two commits have the same one.

sections.instruction: When you have many commits, the full log can be overwhelming. There's a compact view.
sections.prompt: Type `git log --oneline` for a shorter view.

onSuccess.message: One line per commit — just the short hash and the message. Much easier to scan when you have many commits. This is the view most developers use day-to-day.

sections.instruction: Sort these git commands into the correct order for the basic workflow.

completionMessage: You can now view your project's entire history with `git log`. Every change, every message, every save point — all recorded. You've learned the fundamentals of git: init, add, commit, and log.
